blocks.text: Evening and Morning Star

With respect I address a few lines to you in this time of confusion among us,” wrote William W. Phelps to Joseph Smith from Missouri about the loss of the Independence print shop. “Although the enemy has accomplished his design in demolishing the Printing establishment they cannot demolis[h] the design of our God, for his decrees will stand & his purposes must be accomplished.”

Even before Joseph Smith learned the print shop had been destroyed, he received a revelation directing that a lot in Kirtland, Ohio, be dedicated “for the work of the printing of the translation of my scriptures, and all things whatsoever I shall command you.”

A week later, on August 9, 1833, Oliver Cowdery arrived in Kirtland from Missouri, and in October he left for New York to obtain a press and type for the new Kirtland print shop. It took his efforts and those of others to get the new equipment safely to its destination “in the midst of mobs.”

As in Missouri, opposition toward the Church had arisen in Ohio. “The inhabitants of this county threaten our destruction,” Joseph wrote on December 5, “and we know not how soon they may be permitted to follow the example of the Missourians but our trust is in God.”
Despite these challenges, a print shop was established in Kirtland, first down on the flats below where the temple would rise and later on a lot just behind the one for the temple.

In December, The Evening and the Morning Star again appeared, this time printed on the new Kirtland press. On the front page of the newspaper, Oliver Cowdery reflected on the persecution that had led to a five-month break in the paper’s publication.

Initially, Church leaders hoped the governor of Missouri would restore the Saints to their property in Jackson County and that the newspaper would resume publication there. After the 1834 march of “the Camp of Israel”—later known as Zion’s Camp—it became clear that they would not be returning to Jackson County any time soon.

In September 1834, The Evening and the Morning Star announced this issue was its last. With the redemption of Zion now postponed, it made sense to discontinue the publication of the Star and replace it with a new monthly newspaper, The Latter Day Saints’ Messenger and Advocate, which would be printed in a smaller format for easier binding later.

But the last issue of The Evening and the Morning Star also carried a prospectus announcing that the past issues of the Star would be republished in the same format as the Messenger and Advocate. Oliver Cowdery, editor of the Star, encouraged subscribers for the reprint, reminding them that the original Star published in Missouri “was enriched with some of the most important, instructing, and sublime Revelations which have been given in the last days.”

In reprinting the past issues of the Star, Oliver promised to correct the “many typographical errors” in them. He noted “that in the first 14 numbers, in the Revelations, are many errors, typographical, and other, occasioned by transcribing manuscript; but as we shall have access to originals, we shall endeavor to make proper corrections.” He pledged the print shop staff “to furnish their subscribers with, at least, two numbers per month, until the whole are completed,” writing that “with their present arrangements they calculate more.”

The Messenger and Advocate appeared in October 1834, as advertised. The Star reprint, however, did not emerge until January. When it did, Oliver Cowdery included a statement about revisions in the revelations:

“On the revelations we merely say, that we were not a little surprised to find the previous print so different from the original. We have given them a careful comparison, assisted by individuals whose known integrity and ability is uncensurable. Thus saying we cast no reflections on those who were entrusted with the responsibility of publishing them in Missouri, as our own labors were included in that important service to the church, and it was our unceasing endeavor to have them correspond with the copy furnished us. We believe they are now correct. If not in every word, at least in principle. For the special good of the church we have also added a few other items from other revelations.”
As Oliver’s notice suggested, the reissued Star was not a true reprint but rather a revision of the original, and he made changes to the contents of many issues.

Some of the most significant changes were in the revelations. They not only corrected grammar, spelling, and typesetting errors but also included substantial content revisions made with the authorization of Joseph Smith. As one scholar has noted, the content revisions “mainly reflect additions to the Church’s governmental structure and adjustments in the practice of the law of consecration”—the Church’s early economic system.

The Latter-day Saint concept of revelation as taught by Joseph Smith allowed for such changes. Joseph taught that there were many spiritual gifts, including “revelation, visions,” and translation. When he reported having visions, he sometimes recalled specific words he heard in them. At times, “revelation” in this sense differed from visions in that it might come as a feeling or impression instead of a visual experience. He spoke of “noticing the first intimation of the Spirit of Revelation . . . when you feel pure intelligence flowing unto you” that “may give you sudden strokes of ideas.”

Translation was another form of revelation that took place by “the gift and power of God.” The manuscripts of the Book of Mormon show comparatively few changes, suggesting that the revealed words flowed quite freely during its translation.

Joseph’s closest associates sensed differences among these spiritual gifts. In 1839, six members of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles signed their names to a document that described “glad tidings . . . from heaven” coming “by the ministering of angels, by the gift of the Holy Ghost, and by the power of God.”

Yet even these distinctions blurred in practice. Though the words heard in visions might simply be repeated, the visual parts still had to be described in language. In describing one of his best-known visionary experiences, Joseph Smith recalled that “while we were yet in the Spirit, the Lord commanded us that we should write the vision.”

The “sudden strokes of ideas” that came through revelation likewise had to be expressed in language. Orson Pratt explained, “Joseph the Prophet in writing the Doctrine & . . . Covenants, received the ideas from God, but clothed those ideas with such words as came to his mind.”

Even translation was not an automatic process. Oliver Cowdery once sought to translate, thinking that it required “no thought” except to ask God. In a gentle rebuff, a revelation through Joseph Smith taught Oliver that he “must study it out” in his mind first, after which confirmation would come as “feelings.”

On another occasion, Joseph Smith cited an Old Testament scripture and then wrote, “I might have rendered a plainer translation to this, but it is sufficiently plain to suit my purpose as it stands,” suggesting that changes in audience or situation might call for varied wording.

Joseph’s close contemporaries recognized such flexibility in the concept of revelation. John Taylor, an apostle who later became Church president, said that Joseph had the authority “to give a portion of a revelation at one time and then add to it afterwards.”

Orson Pratt described how Joseph inserted paragraphs into recorded revelations years later. Joseph, Orson said, “was inspired in several instances to write additional sentences and paragraphs to the earlier revelations.” In this way, “the Lord did truly give ‘line upon line, here a little and there a little,’ the same as He did to a revelation that Jeremiah received, which after being burned by the wicked king of Israel, the Lord revealed over again with great numbers of additional words.”

Subscribers to the Evening and Morning Star reprint, eager to have copies of the revelations, may have expected the issues of the reprint to appear on a monthly basis. But the reprinting had to fit in with an even higher priority: preparing the corrected revelations for publication in book form.
